description: Cross-registry resolution MUST reject HTTP redirects whose target authority differs from the original request's authority. A cooperating attacker server can pass the IP-range filter on its own public IP and then issue a 30x redirect to 127.0.0.1 or 169.254.169.254 (or any cross-org authority); the cross-authority redirect rule blocks this without re-running the full filter chain on the redirect target.

input.scenario: Resolver fetches https://attacker.example.com/.well-known/acdp.json; server returns a 30x with Location header pointing to a different authority

expected.behavior: Resolver MUST refuse to follow the redirect. The check MUST be a string comparison on the authority (host + port) component of the URL — same-host AND same-port. Path/query/scheme changes within the same authority MAY be followed up to the §7.5 cap of 3 follows. A second concern: even if the redirect chain stays on the same authority, the IP MUST remain pinned (the §7.6 DNS-rebinding protection); a same-authority redirect MUST NOT trigger a fresh DNS lookup.
expected.rationale: Without same-authority enforcement, an attacker who controls a public-facing host they registered with the resolver can use it as a bounce point to any internal address — bypassing the IP-range filter that runs only on the *initial* DNS resolution.
